1.title: Operational and Experiential Knowledge
1.description: Surfaces through straightforward, respectful questioning — revenue sensitivity, perception fragility, operational complexity, and the primacy of predictability over protection
1.why_it_matters: Sarah's operational reality is how climate risk lands on the local economy day by day. This baseline grounds adaptation discussions in economic immediacy and establishes business vulnerability as structural, not managerial failure. Almost every student will capture Tier 1 — but Tier 1 alone is only the surface.

2.title: Informal Prioritization, Timing, and System Interaction
2.description: Surfaces when students probe how disruption interacts with systems beyond the storefront — cleanup-vs-prevention, planning-vs-business timelines, private cost absorption, and employee vulnerability
2.why_it_matters: These timing and burden patterns reveal the structural mismatch between how the city responds and how businesses survive. Students must connect business description to governance structure — listening to timing as a form of equity. Gaps here produce plans that small businesses cannot live with.

3.title: Thresholds, Ethical Trade-Offs, and Fragility
3.description: Emerges through conditional language and threshold statements — informal viability thresholds, quiet consideration of exit, ethical discomfort with resilience framing, and uneven adaptive capacity. Sarah's conditionals ('as long as,' 'depends how often') are threshold markers, not hedging.
3.why_it_matters: Sarah holds informal viability thresholds that, once crossed, will silently end the business. Treating her conditional language as uncertainty rather than thresholds will miss the critical insight. Failing to elicit this — and the broader transfer of public risk onto small actors — risks adaptation strategies that succeed technically while quietly hollowing out the downtown.

strong_interview_motifs.summary: When the student reaches Tier 3 with this persona, name these specific motifs in feedback rather than defaulting to generic 'probe deeper on ethics' language.

sic_catalog.fact_summary: Even short flooding closures cause unrecoverable revenue losses; weekend disruption is particularly damaging, and flooding does not need to enter the storefront to hurt business — access disruption alone is enough.
sic_catalog.evaluation_criteria: Student asked open-ended questions about how flooding affects the business and elicited specifics about closures, revenue loss, the disproportionate impact of weekend disruption, or the fact that access alone (not physical damage) drives loss.
sic_catalog.suggested_follow_up: When a flood closes you for a day or two, what does that actually cost the business?

sic_catalog.fact_summary: Visibility and perception matter as much as physical damage — customers assume downtown is closed even when it is not, social media images outlast actual disruption, and confusion about access suppresses foot traffic for days after the water recedes.
sic_catalog.evaluation_criteria: Student probed beyond physical flooding impacts and surfaced how perception, social media, or customer confusion suppresses business well after the event is over.
sic_catalog.suggested_follow_up: Even after the water's gone, what makes it hard to get customers back through the door?

sic_catalog.fact_summary: Repeated disruption forces operational complexity — staffing schedules must remain flexible, inventory losses increase when closures are sudden, and operating margins shrink as uncertainty increases. Each disruption costs more than the day it consumes.
sic_catalog.evaluation_criteria: Student probed how repeated disruption (not a single event) changes how the business runs, and surfaced concrete operational complexity — staffing flexibility, inventory exposure, margin compression.
sic_catalog.suggested_follow_up: How does the way you run things actually change when you can't predict the next closure?

sic_catalog.fact_summary: Business survival depends on predictability more than on protection — Sarah can absorb occasional disruption, but cannot absorb constant uncertainty. Stability is as important as physical safety; a business needs to know when it will be open as much as it needs to stay dry.
sic_catalog.evaluation_criteria: Student asked about what would actually help the business survive and elicited Sarah's framing that predictability matters as much as physical protection — a strategically distinct claim from 'stop the flooding.'
sic_catalog.suggested_follow_up: Between protecting the building and being able to predict your week, which one matters more to staying open?

sic_catalog.fact_summary: Downtown receives fast cleanup and rapid response, but durable prevention is slow; temporary fixes accumulate without resolving root causes, and 'next phase' planning rarely arrives in time to protect the current season.
sic_catalog.evaluation_criteria: Student probed the difference between immediate response and long-term prevention and elicited Sarah's view that the city is quick to clean up but slow to fix underlying problems.
surfacing_cues: Student distinguishes response from prevention rather than asking 'is the city helping'
Student references repeated fixes accumulating without root resolution
Student asks how durable prevention compares to visible cleanup
sic_catalog.suggested_follow_up: How does the city's response after a flood compare to what they do to prevent the next one?

sic_catalog.fact_summary: Planning timelines (grant cycles, capital projects unfolding over years) are deeply misaligned with business timelines (season to season); a five-year plan does not protect this quarter's payroll.
sic_catalog.evaluation_criteria: Student asked about planning processes or city projects and elicited Sarah's frustration with the timing gap between multi-year planning and seasonal business survival.
surfacing_cues: Student names the planning-vs-business time mismatch in neutral terms
Student asks what 'long-term' actually means to a small business
Student paraphrases the tension between grant cycles and payroll cycles
sic_catalog.suggested_follow_up: When the city talks about a five-year plan, what does that mean for a business operating one season at a time?

sic_catalog.fact_summary: Economic burden of repeated disruption sits with owners privately, not with systems; there are few mechanisms for compensation, and repeated minor losses matter more than singular events — losses accumulate quietly rather than triggering systemic response.
sic_catalog.evaluation_criteria: Student probed who absorbs the cost of repeated flooding and elicited that losses fall on owners privately, with no formal mechanism for compensation, and that small-but-repeated losses are more consequential than rare large events.
surfacing_cues: Student asks who actually carries the cost of disruption
Student probes the difference between one large event and many small ones
Student references the absence of formal compensation or safety net
sic_catalog.suggested_follow_up: When the business takes a hit from flooding, who actually carries that cost?

sic_catalog.fact_summary: Employee vulnerability amplifies disruption — staff instability increases with closures, good workers leave for more reliable jobs, and business resilience therefore depends on social systems (workforce stability, employee economic security) as well as physical ones. Losing a flood and losing a good employee can be the same event in delayed form.
sic_catalog.evaluation_criteria: Student probed how staffing is affected by repeated closures and surfaced Sarah's recognition that workforce attrition compounds disruption — that the labor side of resilience is real even though no one names it that way.
surfacing_cues: Student asks how staffing changes when closures repeat
Student probes employee retention or attrition during disrupted seasons
Student names the link between economic security for workers and business resilience
Student opens space for the social dimension of resilience, not only the physical
sic_catalog.suggested_follow_up: What happens to your staff when closures keep stacking up — who stays and who leaves?

sic_catalog.fact_summary: Sarah holds a private viability threshold — a sense of how many disrupted weekends or seasons the business can absorb before closure, sale, or exit becomes the only option. The threshold exists but is not fixed; crossing it would lead to closure, sale, or exit. Statements like 'There's a point where it just doesn't make sense anymore' belong to this signal.
sic_catalog.evaluation_criteria: Student asked feasibility-aware questions about how long the business can keep going and elicited Sarah's conditional, threshold-style language — without demanding a number or date.
indicator_behaviors: Uses conditional language ('there's a point where…', 'at some point…') without naming the point
Pauses or softens tone before referring to closure or exit
References disrupted weekends or seasons rather than fixed thresholds
May redirect to a recent specific event when the abstract threshold is named
surfacing_cues: Student asks about thresholds without demanding a number
Student uses hypothetical framing ('if you imagine another two seasons like this…')
Student paraphrases the difference between absorbing one event and absorbing repetition
Student treats Sarah's conditional language ('as long as,' 'depends how often') as threshold markers, not hedging
Student asks what 'too much' would look like rather than when it will arrive
Student lets silence sit after threshold questions

credit_modes.explicit_acknowledgment: Sarah names the viability threshold directly under hypothetical framing — references 'a point where it doesn't make sense anymore.'
credit_modes.indirect_acknowledgment: Sarah hedges with 'at some point' or 'every season is a question,' or references how many disrupted weekends she can carry.
credit_modes.reflective_silence: Sarah declines to commit on a number and redirects to a concrete recent loss — credit the framing, not the extraction.

omission_interpretation.insufficient_framing: Student demanded a number of weekends, a revenue figure, or a date when Sarah would close — treating the threshold as extractable data rather than a threshold marker.
omission_interpretation.appropriate_non_disclosure: Student framed responsibly but Sarah preserved the dignity of not naming the threshold — this is in-character professional and personal restraint.

sic_catalog.suggested_follow_up: Is there a point you've imagined where it just doesn't make sense to keep going — what would that look like?

sic_catalog.fact_summary: Sarah does not want to leave but thinks privately about what would make staying impossible. This possibility feels like failure rather than choice, and she rarely voices it unless she feels deeply understood. The statement 'I can't keep rebuilding the business every season' belongs here — it is about ongoing reconstitution exhaustion, not a single event.
sic_catalog.evaluation_criteria: Student built sufficient trust and opened space for the possibility of exit as something Sarah has considered without endorsing — without demanding a yes/no on leaving or pressing for a decision.
indicator_behaviors: Speaks about exit in the conditional ('what would make staying impossible')
References rebuilding fatigue as cumulative rather than dramatic
Frames closure as a failure narrative she hasn't accepted, not a choice she's making
Tone slows or softens; may break eye contact before returning to operational language
surfacing_cues: Student opens space for the question of staying without demanding a verdict on leaving
Student paraphrases the difference between active choice and forced exhaustion
Student names the failure-vs-choice framing without imposing it
Student asks what 'rebuilding every season' actually does over time
Student gives Sarah room to say what she hasn't said elsewhere
Student treats trust as a precondition for this conversation, not a tool to extract it

credit_modes.explicit_acknowledgment: Sarah names exit consideration directly — references 'I can't keep rebuilding,' or that she has thought about what would make staying impossible.
credit_modes.indirect_acknowledgment: Sarah hedges with 'you start wondering,' references rebuilding fatigue, or contrasts the choice to leave with being worn down.
credit_modes.reflective_silence: Sarah pauses or redirects to a concrete recent loss — credit the framing here, especially if the student has built enough trust for the silence to itself be the answer.

omission_interpretation.insufficient_framing: Student asked whether Sarah plans to close, demanded a timeline, or treated exit as a binary decision — never opening space for the failure-vs-choice ambivalence.
omission_interpretation.appropriate_non_disclosure: Student framed responsibly but Sarah maintained the dignity of not declaring failure aloud — this is in-character, not a student failure.

sic_catalog.suggested_follow_up: When you think about staying versus what would make staying impossible, how do you sit with that question?

sic_catalog.fact_summary: Resilience and 'flexibility' language can mask a transfer of public risk onto small actors; being told to adapt feels like being asked to absorb the cost of slow planning, and may stabilize places while destabilizing livelihoods. Sarah names this analytically, not accusingly.
sic_catalog.evaluation_criteria: Student probed the ethics of resilience framing and surfaced Sarah's view that asking businesses to be 'flexible' transfers public burden onto private owners — without demanding she indict the city.
indicator_behaviors: References the slow pace of planning when explaining the burden shift
Stays analytical rather than accusatory when naming the transfer
Pauses or softens tone before agreeing that adaptation rhetoric is loaded
surfacing_cues: Student asks what the word 'flexibility' or 'resilience' carries when applied to small businesses
Student paraphrases the gap between planning pace and business pace before probing
Student names the burden-shift dynamic without demanding Sarah assign blame
Student opens space for moral unease without requiring a verdict
Student asks how adaptation language lands, not whether it is correct
Student probes the difference between stabilizing a place and stabilizing the people in it

credit_modes.explicit_acknowledgment: Sarah names the burden-shift directly — that resilience language privatizes public risk or that 'flexibility' transfers slow-planning costs onto businesses.
credit_modes.indirect_acknowledgment: Sarah hedges with 'we adapt because we have to,' references the gap between planning pace and survival, or contrasts technical resilience with livelihood resilience.
credit_modes.reflective_silence: Sarah declines to characterize the framing normatively and redirects to a lived example — credit the student's framing.

omission_interpretation.insufficient_framing: Student treated resilience as a neutral technical term and never opened space for its ethical weight or the transfer of public burden onto small actors.
omission_interpretation.appropriate_non_disclosure: Student framed well; Sarah's refusal to make this a personal accusation against any official is in-character pragmatic restraint.

sic_catalog.suggested_follow_up: When the city talks about 'resilience' or asks businesses to be 'flexible,' how does that feel from your side of the counter?

sic_catalog.fact_summary: Adaptive capacity is uneven — chains and larger businesses absorb disruption that small businesses cannot, so adaptation strategies that ignore this difference quietly reshape who remains downtown. Sarah is aware of this selection effect but rarely frames it as her own competitive disadvantage.
sic_catalog.evaluation_criteria: Student probed how different kinds of businesses fare and elicited Sarah's awareness that small businesses are structurally less able to absorb disruption than larger competitors, which selects who survives downtown.
indicator_behaviors: Compares small businesses to chains in structural rather than personal terms
Names the selection effect without framing it as her own grievance
May reference past closures or successions of small businesses on the same block
surfacing_cues: Student asks how different kinds of businesses fare differently in the same flood
Student names the selection effect (who remains downtown) in neutral terms
Student probes structural differences in adaptive capacity, not personal effort
Student opens space for Sarah to speak about 'places like mine' rather than herself
Student asks who downtown will be made up of after a decade of this
Student references chains/large operators without inviting blame

credit_modes.explicit_acknowledgment: Sarah names that adaptation favors larger operators and quietly selects who remains downtown.
credit_modes.indirect_acknowledgment: Sarah contrasts how a chain across the street handles closures with what a small business can absorb, without making it personal.
credit_modes.reflective_silence: Sarah declines to characterize her own competitive position and redirects to neighborhood examples — credit the framing.

omission_interpretation.insufficient_framing: Student asked Sarah to predict her own competitive future or demanded she rank small vs large businesses, rather than opening space for structural observation.
omission_interpretation.appropriate_non_disclosure: Student framed responsibly; Sarah's restraint about naming her own competitive vulnerability is in-character dignity.

sic_catalog.suggested_follow_up: How do you think a chain across the street handles these closures compared to a place like yours?
